questions.question: What type of element is the <div> tag in HTML?
questions.explanation: The <div> element is a block-level container that takes up the full width available and starts on a new line. It is used to group other elements together for styling or layout purposes.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the <div> element?
choices: To group content together as a generic container
To define a paragraph of text
questions.explanation: The <div> element is a generic container used to group content together. It has no inherent semantic meaning and is primarily used for applying CSS styles or JavaScript behavior to a section of content.

questions.question: Why is it generally better to use semantic elements like <article> or <section> instead of <div>?
choices: Semantic elements load faster than <div> elements
Semantic elements provide meaning about the content, improving accessibility and SEO
The <div> element is deprecated in HTML5
Semantic elements automatically apply default styling
questions.explanation: Semantic elements convey meaning about the content they contain, which helps screen readers, search engines, and other tools understand the page structure. <div> is appropriate when no semantic element fits the purpose.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid reason to use a <div> element?
choices: To wrap elements for CSS layout purposes
To group elements for applying a shared style
To represent a standalone section of content with a heading
To serve as a container for JavaScript DOM manipulation
questions.explanation: A standalone section of content with a heading should use the <section> or <article> element, not a <div>. The <div> should be used only when no semantic element is appropriate, typically for layout or styling purposes.

questions.question: The <div> element is a ___-level container that starts on a new line and takes up the full width available.
questions.explanation: The <div> is a block-level element, meaning it starts on a new line and stretches to fill the full width of its parent container by default. This is in contrast to inline elements like <span>.

questions.question: The inline equivalent of the <div> element is the ___ element.
questions.explanation: While <div> is a block-level generic container, <span> is an inline generic container. <span> does not start on a new line and only takes up as much width as its content requires.
